flashcards.Back: A globally unique and routable IPv6 address, equivalent to a public IPv4 address. gua addresses currently begin with the binary prefix 001 (2000::/3).
An IPv6 address intended for use within an organization's internal network, similar to private IPv4. They use the prefix fc00::/7 (commonly fd00::/8).
An IPv6 address used for communication on a single local link. Every IPv6-enabled interface must have an LLA, which starts with fe80::/10.
An address used to send a single packet to multiple destinations. These addresses always begin with the prefix ff00::/8.
An IPv6 address assigned to multiple nodes. A packet sent to this address is delivered to the 'nearest' node as determined by the routing protocol.
A specialized multicast address used in NDP to efficiently resolve MAC addresses. it is formed by ff02::1:ff00:0/104 + the last 24 bits of the unicast address.
A reserved IPv6 address that targets every IPv6-enabled device on the local link, serving as the replacement for the IPv4 broadcast.
A reserved IPv6 address used to communicate with all routers on a segment, primarily for Neighbor Discovery functions.
A migration strategy where network devices run both IPv4 and IPv6 protocol stacks simultaneously to ensure compatibility during the transition.
A transition method that encapsulates IPv6 packets inside IPv4 headers so they can be transported across an existing IPv4-only infrastructure.
The range of reachability for an address. Examples include link-local (one segment) and global (the entire internet).
A multicast scope (ff01::/16) that limits traffic to the individual interface itself, primarily used for loopback testing.
A legacy IPv6 address scope that was intended to limit traffic to a single physical site. it has been replaced by Unique Local Addresses.
A multicast scope that limits traffic to all nodes within a specific organization, spanning multiple sites.
A fixed 40-byte header that is simpler than the IPv4 header, allowing for faster processing by routers.
The reserved IPv6 address 0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1, used by a host to send packets to its own software stack for testing.
The all-zeros IPv6 address (::/128) used as a temporary source address by a host before it has been assigned a unique address.
The reserved multicast address used by OSPFv3 to communicate with all SPF routers on a segment.
The reserved multicast address used by OSPFv3 to communicate with the Designated Router (DR) and Backup Designated Router (BDR).
In IPv6 routing, the next-hop address is typically the Link-Local Address of the adjacent router interface.
The specific algorithm of splitting a MAC, inserting 'fffe', and flipping the 7th bit to create a 64-bit interface ID.
The process in EUI-64 where the Universal/Local bit of the MAC address is inverted to indicate that the resulting interface ID is universally unique.
A 40-bit random value within a Unique Local Address that ensures the address block is highly likely to be unique across different organizations.
A single character (0-9, A-F) representing 4 bits of data. 32 of these digits make up a full IPv6 address.
The message type code for a Neighbor Solicitation (NS), used to discover a neighbor's MAC address.
The message type code for a Neighbor Advertisement (NA), used to respond to address resolution requests.
The boundary in a public address that separates the ISP-assigned prefix from the organization's internal subnets and host interface IDs.
A set of reserved multicast addresses (like ff02::1) that have a predefined purpose and are recognized by all IPv6-compliant devices.
A link-local address that is automatically generated by the device hardware or OS using EUI-64 or a randomized interface ID.
A link-local address that is manually configured by an administrator to simplify routing protocol peerings and troubleshooting.
